title: Spiced hot cross buns
subtitle: Good Friday yeasted buns with currants, mixed peel and a piped cross
excerpt: British hot cross buns in the Good Friday tradition: a soft yeasted dough scented with mixed spice and cinnamon, studded with currants and mixed peel, marked with a flour-paste cross, and brushed with sticky sugar syrup straight from the oven. Twelve buns.
sourceNotes: Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): documents the hot cross bun in the Easter chapter. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: gives the spiced dough with currants and peel. The piped flour-paste cross (in place of a knife-cut cross) is the modern British bakery form documented from the late nineteenth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Bake, cool, freeze in a tin; warm in a 160°C oven for 5 minutes to refresh.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cold-proof the shaped buns overnight in the fridge; bake from cold in the morning.

content.text: Hot cross buns are a British Good Friday bake: a spiced yeasted dough enriched with butter, milk and egg, studded with currants and mixed peel, shaped into rounds, marked with a piped flour-paste cross, and finished with a hot sugar glaze. The buns split when broken in half, releasing the smell of cinnamon, nutmeg and mixed spice.
Best on the day they are baked, but they toast well on the second and third day, which is why they survived as a tradition.

Mix the first {{plain-flour}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, {{mixed-spice}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, {{nutmeg-ground}} and {{yeast-fast-action}} in a stand-mixer bowl. Add the lukewarm {{whole-milk}}, softened {{unsalted-butter}} and {{eggs}}. Mix on low for 7 minutes to a soft dough. Add the {{dried-currants}} and {{mixed-peel}}; mix briefly to incorporate. Bulk-ferment in an oiled bowl for 1 hour until doubled.

Knock back and divide into 12 equal pieces (about 90 g each). Shape each into a tight ball and set on a lined tray, 3 cm apart, in a 4×3 grid. Proof for 90 minutes until puffed and the buns are touching. Heat the oven to 200°C fan. For the cross: whisk the second {{plain-flour}} with the first {{water}} to a smooth thick paste. Pipe a cross over each bun.

Bake for 18–20 minutes; the buns should be deep gold and the crosses should be set and pale. Dissolve the second {{caster-sugar}} in the second {{water}} in a small pan and bring to a simmer for 2 minutes. Brush the syrup generously over the hot buns as soon as they come out. Cool on a rack.
